learningGoal: How to order values from smallest to largest
How to find the middle value in an ordered list

screens.title: What's the middle value?
screens.body:
Ava recorded the number of books she read each month: **7, 3, 5, 9, 1** books.
The **median** is the middle value — but you must put the numbers in **order** first!

screens.title: Step by step: find the median

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Order first, count, then find the middle. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — you must put values in order first, then count them, then find the middle one.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Ava now has a new set of data: **6, 2, 8, 4, 10** books. What is the median?

interaction.question: What is the median of 2, 4, 6, 8, 10?
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! The middle value is **6 books**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Put the values in order: 2, 4, 6, 8, 10. The middle value (3th out of 5) is **6**.

steps.why: Values in order, smallest to largest.

screens.title: The median recipe!
screens.body: The median is the **middle value** when the data is in **order**:

steps.text: Step 1: Put all values in order (smallest to largest)
steps.why: This is the step most people forget!

steps.text: Step 2: Count how many values there are
steps.why: You need to know whether the count is odd or even.

steps.text: Step 3 (odd count): Pick the middle value
steps.why: Cross off one from each end until one is left. ✓

steps.text: Step 4 (even count): Find the mean of the two middle values
steps.why: Add the two middle values and divide by 2. ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid finding the median (the middle value) without ordering first
Why the order matters for finding the middle

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body: Charlie has test scores: 8, 3, 6, 1, 5. They said: "The middle number is **6**, so the median is 6." But that's wrong! Can you see why?

steps.why: Charlie picked 6 from this list.
Is this really the middle value?

screens.title: You MUST order the values first!
Your turn!
screens.body: New values: **9, 2, 7, 4, 11**. What is the median?

interaction.question: What is the median?
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! The ordered list is 2, 4, 7, 9, 11 and the middle value is **7**. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Order first: 2, 4, 7, 9, 11. The middle value is **7**.

steps.why: Find the middle of THIS list.

screens.title: Order first — always!
screens.body: The number one mistake with medians: forgetting to order the values.

steps.text: Step 1: Put values in ORDER (smallest to largest)
steps.why: Never skip this step!

steps.text: Step 2: Cross off from each end to find the middle
steps.why: Cross off the smallest and the largest, then repeat.

steps.text: The last one left is the median!
steps.why: It's the true middle of the ordered data. ✓

learningGoal: How to find the most frequent value in a data set
Why the mode (the most common value) is the value itself, not the frequency (how many times it appears)

screens.title: Which value appears the most?
screens.body:
Ella asked her classmates their favourite number of pets to own: **2, 3, 1, 2, 4, 2, 3, 1, 2**.
The **mode** is the value that appears **most often**. Can you spot it?

screens.title: Count each value — the highest count wins!
Your turn!
screens.body: New data: **3, 1, 4, 3, 2, 3, 1, 3, 5**. What is the mode?

interaction.question: What is the mode?
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **3** appears 4 times — more than any other value. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Count each value. **3** appears 4 times — that's the most, so the mode is **3**.

screens.title: The mode is the MOST!
screens.body: **MO**de is the **MO**st com**MO**n — all three words have **MO** in them! That's your memory trick.

steps.why: All three words share 'MO' — easy to remember!
Count how many times each number appears.

steps.text: Step 2: Find the value with the highest count
steps.why: That's the mode!

steps.text: The mode is the VALUE, not the count
steps.why: Don't say 'the mode is 4 times' — say 'the mode is 5'. ✓

learningGoal: Why the mode (most common value) can be more useful than the mean (total divided by how many) sometimes
How to handle data with no mode or two modes

screens.title: When the mode beats the mean!
screens.body: Ben asked his classmates their shoe size to help the PE teacher order new trainers. Sometimes you don't need the average — you need to know what's **most popular**. That's exactly what the **mode** tells you!

steps.why: The PE teacher should order more size 5 trainers — that's the most common size!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: New data: **2, 3, 3, 4, 4, 4, 5, 5, 6**. What is the mode?

interaction.question: What is the mode?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The value **4** appears 3 times — more than any other. That's the mode!

screens.title: Mode = Most common!
screens.body: Remember: **Mo**de = **Mo**st. Easy!

steps.text: Mode = the value that appears MOST often
steps.why: Not the count — the actual value!
Great for shoe sizes, favourites, and categories.

steps.text: Some data has NO mode (all equal) or TWO modes
steps.why: That's fine — just say 'no mode' or list both. ✓

learningGoal: How to calculate the range as highest minus lowest
Why the range is a single number, not two numbers

screens.title: How spread out is the data?
screens.body:
Grace recorded daily temperatures for a week: **8, 14, 11, 6, 15, 9, 12** °C.
The **range** tells us how **spread out** the data is. It's the simplest measure there is — just one subtraction!

interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Range = highest minus lowest. One subtraction gives you one number. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — the range is found by **subtracting** the lowest from the highest!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: New data: **10, 16, 13, 5, 17, 7, 14** °C. What is the range?

interaction.question: What is the range?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Highest (17) minus lowest (5) = **12**.

screens.title: One subtraction — that's it!
screens.body: The range is the easiest measure in data handling:

steps.why: The biggest number in the data.
The smallest number in the data.
Give ONE number, not 'from 6 to 15'. ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid giving two values instead of one for the range
Why the range is a subtraction, not a pair of numbers

screens.title: Close — but not quite!
screens.body: Tom has test scores: 4, 9, 6, 3, 8. They wrote: "The range is **3 to 9**." Is that right?

steps.why: Is this a valid answer?

screens.title: The range is ONE number, not two!
Your turn!
screens.body: New data: **5, 12, 7, 2, 10**. What is the range?

interaction.question: What is the range?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Range = highest − lowest = 12 − 2 = **10**.

screens.title: Range = one subtraction!
screens.body: The range is always a **single number**:

steps.why: Always subtract!

steps.text: Give ONE number as your answer
steps.why: Not '3 to 9' — just write '6'.

steps.text: It measures how SPREAD OUT the data is
steps.why: Big range = spread out. Small range = clustered. ✓

learningGoal: How to read values from a bar chart (a chart that uses bars to show data) accurately
Why checking the scale is important before reading bars

screens.title: What does the bar chart show?
screens.body:
Ella collected data on favourite fruits in her class. Look at the bar chart below.
Before reading any values, always check the **scale** on the axis!

screens.title: Check the scale, then read carefully
Your turn!
screens.body: Using the bar chart, work out **how many more chose Apple than Grape**.

interaction.question: How many more chose Apple than Grape?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Read the values carefully from the chart. The answer is **5**.

screens.title: Bar chart reading tips!
screens.body: Before answering any bar chart question:

steps.text: Step 1: Check the SCALE on the axis
steps.why: What does each grid line represent? 1? 2? 5? 10?

steps.text: Step 2: Read the TOP of each bar against the scale
steps.why: Line it up carefully — don't guess!

steps.text: Step 3: Answer what the question ASKS
steps.why: 'How many more' = subtract. 'How many altogether' = add. ✓

learningGoal: How to spot when bar charts are misleading because the scale is tricky
When to use bar charts versus other types of chart

screens.title: Don't let the chart trick you!
screens.body: Finn is comparing two bar charts of the same data — one starts at 0 and one starts at 50. Bar charts can be **misleading** if you don't check the scale first. Starting at 50 makes the differences look HUGE, but they're actually small.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: From the chart below, **what is the range of the scores**?

interaction.question: What is the range of the scores?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Read the values carefully. The answer is **8**.

screens.title: Bar chart survival guide!
screens.body: Remember these tips every time you see a bar chart:

steps.text: Check the axis labels and scale FIRST
steps.why: Know what each grid line is worth.
If not, be extra careful — the bars might be misleading.

steps.text: Read the NUMBER, don't estimate by eye
steps.why: Trust the scale, not the bar height. ✓

learningGoal: How to read line graphs that show change over time
How to read values and spot trends on a line graph

screens.title: What's the pattern?
screens.body:
Sophie tracked the temperature outside school each hour. The line graph shows:
**9am=8°C, 10am=10°C, 11am=13°C, 12pm=15°C, 1pm=14°C, 2pm=12°C**
Line graphs are brilliant for showing how things **change over time**.

screens.title: Reading line graphs: values and trends
Your turn!
screens.body: From the line graph, **what was the range of temperatures**?

interaction.question: What was the range of temperatures?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Check the data carefully. The answer is **7**.

screens.title: Line graph reading tips!
screens.body: Line graphs show **change over time**. Remember:

steps.why: The steeper the line, the faster the increase.
A steep drop means a rapid decrease.
The value stayed the same between those points. ✓

learningGoal: How to spot the steepest section of a line graph
Why the steepest part shows the biggest change

screens.title: Where's the steepest bit?
screens.body: Charlie is watching how water temperature changes when heated. The **steepest part** of a line graph shows where the **biggest change** happened. Can you spot it?

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: From the line graph, **what was the total temperature rise**?

interaction.question: What was the total temperature rise?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Check the data carefully. The answer is **25**.

screens.title: Reading line graphs like a pro!
screens.body: Line graphs are all about **change over time**:

steps.why: Look for the part where the line rises or falls fastest.
The line barely moves — things stayed roughly the same.

steps.text: To find total change: last value − first value
steps.why: Subtract the start from the end. ✓

learningGoal: How to calculate values from a pie chart using fractions and degrees
How to find the angle for a section when you know the fraction

screens.title: What do the slices mean?
screens.body: Ava has a pie chart showing how 60 pupils travel to school. A pie chart shows **fractions of the whole**. The full circle = **360°** = **all 60 people**. Each slice is a fraction of the total.

screens.title: From fraction to number of people
Your turn!
screens.body: A new pie chart shows **48** people. How many chose **Car**?

interaction.question: How many chose Car?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Car = 1/3 of 48 = **16**.

screens.title: Pie chart reading recipe!
screens.body: Pie charts show **fractions of a total**. Here's how to read them:

steps.text: Step 1: Find the TOTAL (how many people/things altogether)
steps.why: The whole pie = the total.

steps.text: Step 2: Read the FRACTION (or angle) for each slice
steps.why: 360° = the whole. 180° = half. 90° = quarter.
That gives you the actual number for that slice. ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid confusing the angle with the number of people
Why 90° does not always mean 90 people

screens.title: Can you spot the mistake?
screens.body: Tom sees a 90° slice in a pie chart of 80 people. They wrote: "The number of people = **90**." But the total is only 80! What went wrong?

interaction.sentence: To find the number of people, convert the angle to a ____ first
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Convert the angle to a fraction (angle ÷ 360), then multiply by the total. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — convert the angle to a **fraction** (angle ÷ 360), then multiply by the total!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A 120° slice in a pie chart of **60** people. How many people does it represent?

interaction.question: How many people does the 120° slice represent?

screens.title: Degrees are not counts!
screens.body: The most common pie chart mistake: confusing the **angle** with the **number of people**.

steps.why: This is NOT the answer!
NOW you have the actual number of people. ✓

learningGoal: How to read values from a two-way table
How to find totals and missing values

screens.title: Tables organise data in rows and columns!
screens.body: Lily has a table showing children's pet preferences by gender. Two-way tables let you compare data across **two categories** at once!

visualProps.tableDesc: Boys: Cat=8, Dog=12, Fish=5. Girls: Cat=10, Dog=7, Fish=3.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Use the table to answer: **how many children chose fish altogether**?

interaction.question: How many children chose fish altogether?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Boys fish (4) + Girls fish (5) = 9 = **9**.

visualProps.tableDesc: Boys: Cat=6, Dog=14, Fish=4. Girls: Cat=9, Dog=8, Fish=5.

screens.title: Two-way table tips!
screens.body: Two-way tables are simple once you know how to navigate them:

steps.text: Step 1: Find the RIGHT row and column
steps.why: Use the labels to guide you.

steps.text: Step 2: For totals, add a whole row or column
steps.why: Add all values in that row or column.

steps.text: Step 3: For missing values, use the total to work backwards
steps.why: Total − known values = missing value. ✓

learningGoal: How to find a missing value in a two-way table
When to add and when to subtract

screens.title: Find the missing value!
screens.body: Finn has a table where one value is missing. The row total is **25**. Can you find the **?** value?

visualProps.tableDesc: Boys: Cat=8, Dog=10, Fish=?. Girls: Cat=6, Dog=5, Fish=4.

screens.title: Use the total to find what's missing

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Total, then add known, then subtract. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first find the total, then add the known values, then subtract to find the missing one.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The row total is **28**. Find the **?** value in the table.

visualProps.tableDesc: Boys: Walk=6, Bus=11, Car=?. Girls: Walk=9, Bus=5, Car=3.

screens.title: Missing values in tables!
screens.body: When a table has a gap, use the **totals** to work backwards:

steps.text: Find the total for that row or column
steps.why: This is usually given in the table.

steps.text: Add up the values you DO know
steps.why: These are the filled-in cells.
Simple subtraction gives you the answer. ✓

subConcepts.name: Finding a Missing Number When the Mean is Given

learningGoal: How to work backwards from the mean (total divided by how many) to find a missing value
Why you multiply the mean by the count to find the total

screens.title: Working backwards from the mean!
screens.body: Sophie got scores of 7, 9, and 8 in three tests. Her mean across all four tests is 8. You know the **mean** and all the values except one. Can you find the missing number?

interaction.sentence: To find the total from the mean, you ____ the mean by the count
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Total = mean × count. Then subtract the known values to find the missing one. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — you **multiply** the mean by the count to find the total!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The mean of 4 values is **7**. The known values are **6, 10, 5**. What is the missing value?

interaction.question: What is the missing value?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Total = 7 × 4 = 28. Then 28 − 21 = **7**.

screens.title: The backwards mean recipe!
screens.body: When you know the mean and need to find a missing value:

steps.why: This tells you what all the values should add up to.

steps.text: Step 2: Add up the known values
steps.why: Find the sum of the numbers you already have.
The difference is the missing value. ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid dividing when you should multiply
Why the first step is always Mean x Count

screens.title: Something doesn't add up!
screens.body: Charlie knows the mean of 5 values is 8, and 4 values add to 34. They wrote: "8 ÷ 5 = 1.6" and got stuck. What went wrong?

steps.why: This doesn't look right!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The mean of 5 values is **7**. The known values add to **29**. What's the missing value?

interaction.question: What is the missing value?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Total = 7 × 5 = 35. Then 35 − 29 = **6**.

steps.why: Multiply, not divide!

screens.title: Remember: MULTIPLY to find the total!
screens.body: The first step is always **Mean × Count = Total**. Never divide!

steps.why: MULTIPLY — this undoes the division in the mean formula.
Subtract what you already know.

steps.text: Check: does the mean work with your answer?
steps.why: Add all values, divide by count — you should get the given mean. ✓

subConcepts.name: Median with an Even Number of Values

learningGoal: How to find the median (the middle value) when there is an even number of values
Why you must average the two middle values

screens.title: What if there's no single middle value?
screens.body: Holly has six test scores: **4, 8, 3, 7, 5, 9** marks. That's **6** values — an **even number**. There's no single middle value, so what do we do?

steps.why: 6 values — no single middle!

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Order, find two middles, then average them. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — first order, then find the two middle values, then average them by adding and dividing by 2.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: New values in order: **2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 12**. The two middle values are **6** and **8**. What is the median?

interaction.question: What is the median?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Average the two middles: (6 + 8) ÷ 2 = **7**.

screens.title: Even count? Average the middle two!
screens.body: When the number of values is **even**:

steps.why: Smallest to largest — always!

steps.text: Step 2: Find the two middle values
steps.why: They're the ones either side of the centre.

steps.text: Step 3: Add them and divide by 2
steps.why: Their average is the median. ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid picking just one middle value when the count is even
Why averaging two values gives a more accurate median (middle value)

screens.title: Close — but not quite right!
screens.body: Tom has four values: 3, 6, 9, 14. They said the median is **6**. But with an even number of values, you can't just pick one middle number...

steps.why: 4 values — even number!
Is this correct?

screens.title: You must average BOTH middle values!
Your turn!
screens.body: New values: **4, 7, 11, 16**. The two middle values are 7 and 11. What is the median?

interaction.question: What is the median?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Average both middles: (7 + 11) ÷ 2 = **9**.

screens.title: Even count = average the two middles!
screens.body: This is a classic exam mistake. Remember:

steps.why: Just pick the one in the centre.
Don't just pick one — you need both!

steps.text: Add the two middles and divide by 2
steps.why: That's the median. The answer might be a decimal! ✓

subConcepts.name: Questions Using Mode AND Range Together

learningGoal: How to handle questions that test two different measures at once
When to use mode (most common value) and when to use range (highest minus lowest)

screens.title: Two measures tell you more than one!
screens.body:
Grace has two sets of test scores and needs to compare consistency.
**Set A:** 6, 8, 6, 9, 6
**Set B:** 5, 10, 3, 9, 8
The **mode** tells you the most common value. The **range** tells you how spread out the data is. Together, they paint a full picture!

steps.text: Set B — Mode: no mode, Range: 7
Set A is more consistent (smaller range) and the most common score is 6
steps.why: Mode + range together tell the full story!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: New data set: **4, 7, 2, 9, 3**. What is the range?

interaction.question: What is the range?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Highest (9) − lowest (2) = **7**.

screens.title: Comparing data sets!
screens.body: When exam questions ask you to **compare** data sets, use multiple measures:

steps.text: Mode: which value appears most often?
steps.why: Tells you the most common or most popular result.
Tells you how spread out or consistent the data is.

steps.text: Compare: 'Set A is more consistent because...'
steps.why: Always EXPLAIN using the numbers. ✓

learningGoal: How to decide which average to use in different situations
Why some averages are better than others for certain data

screens.title: Which average should you use?
screens.body: Tom wants to know the most popular shoe size to order for the school shop. There are three main averages: **mean**, **median**, and **mode** (plus the **range** for spread). Which one is best here?

steps.text: Mean: add all, divide by count
steps.why: Best for evenly spread data.
Best when there are extreme values.
Best for 'most popular'.
Shows how spread out or consistent data is.

screens.title: Use the right tool for the job!
Your turn!
screens.body: Tom wants to know the most popular shoe size to order for the school shop. Data: **3, 4, 5, 5, 5, 6, 6, 7**. Calculate the **mode**.

interaction.question: What is the mode?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The mode is **5**. Try calculating it again step by step.

steps.why: Find the mode.

screens.title: The averages song!
screens.body:
Here's a rhyme to help you remember all four:

*Hey diddle diddle, the **Median's** the middle,*
*You add and divide for the **Mean**.*
*The **Mode** is the one that appears the most,*
*And the **Range** is the difference between!*

steps.text: Mean: add all values, divide by count
steps.why: 'You add and divide for the Mean.'
'The Median's the middle.'
'The Mode is the one that appears the most.'
'The Range is the difference between!' ✓
